finding: preprocessMarkdown returned early on any document containing no `[`. `\[` contains one but `\(` does not, so a message whose only markup is inline math never reached the math pass and the entire feature was a silent no-op for its most common real input. Fixed by admitting `\(` in the guard (ITEM-8); pinned by TEST-13, which asserts through preprocessMarkdown rather than the normalizer.

The pre-existing test `the early return still short-circuits delimiter-free input` did not merely miss the bug above — it ASSERTED it as correct (`a string with \( and no [ short-circuits, which is correct now that inline is passthrough`). Every other math test calls normalizeMathDelimiters directly and so cannot see the caller's guard at all. Rewritten to assert conversion; this is the class of hollow coverage that lets a feature ship green and dead.

Injecting a `$` delimiter into a paragraph that already holds an unpaired live `$` lets the two pair up: `cost $5 and \( E \) here` becomes `cost $5 and $E$ here`, which micromark tokenizes as math `5 and ` plus a dangling literal `E$ here`. This is the only way the rewrite can genuinely corrupt output, and the display pass is immune to it (a `$$` run cannot pair with a lone `$`), so it is new surface area. Fixed by paragraphHasLiveDollar; pinned by TEST-5 and e2e TEST-18.

The paragraph guard is part-local. preprocessMarkdown splits on fenced and inline code BEFORE calling the normalizer, so a paragraph interrupted by an inline-code span arrives as two strings and a live `$` on the far side of that span is invisible to the guard. Excluding `$` inside code is correct (it is not live math); the residual case needs an unpaired `$`, an inline-code span and inline math in one paragraph, and degrades to the same mangling markdown already produces for a lone `$` beside any `$…$` span. Accepted and documented in the normalizeInlineMath docblock rather than chased.

Same part-local root cause for the indented-code guard: `lineHead` is derived from the part, so an inline-code span earlier on the line can make a genuinely 4-column-indented line look unindented and defeat the guard. Pre-existing — the display pass computes lineHead identically on the same parts — so this is inherited, not introduced. Documented alongside the paragraph-guard limit.

paragraphHasLiveDollar ran per match, making a paragraph with M inline-math spans O(M x paragraph) on EVERY streaming frame. Fixed by hoisting one O(n) hasLiveDollar(md) scan: with no live `$` anywhere in the string, no paragraph can contain one, so the per-match scan is skipped entirely in the overwhelmingly common case. Exactly equivalent, not an approximation.

The rewritten prose spec asserted only `.katex count > 0`, which is weaker than its TESTS.md claim: any unrelated span converting would satisfy it, and it could not distinguish the sed line converting from the escape-prose line converting. Strengthened to an exact count of 2 plus a negative assertion that `s/(foo)/bar/` — precisely what the DOM showed before this feature — is gone.

Flavor B routes strictly more model-controlled text into KaTeX, so KaTeX's `trust` option matters: with trust enabled, `\href`/`\url`/`\includegraphics` become an injection vector. Verified at the single plugin-construction site (streamdownPlugins.ts:35) that createMathPlugin is called with only singleDollarTextMath, leaving rehype-katex's default `trust: false` and `strict` in force. No vector today; flagged so anyone enabling trust re-reads this.

No throw path exists: the non-string input guard is retained, both regexes are length-bounded, the paragraph scanners are index-clamped (paragraphStart returns 0 and paragraphEnd returns str.length when no boundary is found), and hasLiveDollar's backslash walk is guarded by `i - slashes - 1 >= 0` against underflow. Every guard's failure action is `return whole`, so a malformed input degrades to today's rendering rather than raising.

The exported surface is unchanged: one `normalizeMathDelimiters(md: string): string`. The display pass moved into a private normalizeDisplayMath with byte-identical body, and the new normalizeInlineMath is private, so no caller can invoke the two out of order and break the load-bearing display-then-inline contract. Verified the only production caller (markdownPreprocess.ts:99) is unaffected.

Mirrors the sibling display pass idiom-for-idiom: named module-level regex const with the lookbehind and the length cap explained as a ReDoS bound rather than a style choice, a guard chain whose every branch returns the original text, and reuse of continuationPrefix instead of re-deriving the indented-code test. No new pattern is introduced.

BRE_SIGNAL_RE also skips genuine LaTeX that uses `\{`/`\}` (set braces) or `\|` (norms), e.g. `\( \{1,2\} \)`. This is a deliberate cost of the user-chosen regex guard, not an oversight; it degrades to today's rendering. Recorded in the const's comment so the tradeoff is discoverable rather than mysterious, and no magic value is left unexplained (the 300-char cap likewise states its rationale and its ~6x headroom).

The change is self-contained: two private passes behind one unchanged export, no reach into other modules, no new import, no state. The ordering dependency between the passes is enforced structurally (both are private, composed in one place) rather than by convention across module boundaries.

Adding a third delimiter dialect would mean a third named pass plus its guard chain, composed in the same entry point — an additive edit in one file, not a closed switch that every addition must widen. The guard chain itself is a linear sequence of early returns, so inserting or reordering a guard is local.

Net accessibility improvement: an equation that previously reached the DOM as literal `( \lambda = \sqrt{D/k} )` — backslash control sequences read aloud verbatim — now renders as KaTeX with a MathML `<annotation encoding=application/x-tex>` carrying the source, which is the accessible-name path screen readers consume. The existing specs' convention of NOT asserting raw TeX is absent is preserved for exactly this reason.

Pure string -> string with no store, subscription, cache, memo, or module-level mutable state; the only module-level values are frozen regex literals, and neither pass mutates the input. There is therefore no stale-derivation or invalidation surface, and no Rules-of-Hooks exposure (nothing here is a hook or is called from a render loop reading a store).

No user-facing copy is added or changed — the diff contributes no strings to the UI, only a transformation of model-authored content. Nothing to translate, and no hardcoded English leaks into a component (lint:colors and the biome guardrails pass clean).

The early-return guard was split into two statements (type check, then content check) rather than one compound condition. This is the ergonomic fix as much as the correctness one: the original single-line form invited exactly the misreading its own comment made — that `[` admits every math input — and the split lets the content clause carry its own explanation of why `\(` must be listed separately.

Titles and list labels never reach the markdown renderer, so a `\( m \)` in them surfaced verbatim — the header and every sidebar row showed `Check: the energy is \[ E = mc^2 \] where \( m \) ...`. Reported from the live container after the body was already rendering correctly. Fixed by ITEM-11's mathToPlainText at both label rungs; the preview rung matters most because it IS the user's raw message.

Deliberately returns `string`, not a React node. conversationDisplayLabel feeds an aria-label (ConversationCard.tsx:116) and two search predicates (PaneManagerDrawer.tsx:122, ConversationPickerPane.tsx:48) — none can hold a node — so rendering real KaTeX in a label was never an option. Stripping also makes the search predicate match the text the user actually sees.

The transform is applied AFTER the title-over-preview precedence choice and re-checked with firstNonBlank, so a label whose entire content was a delimiter pair resolves to its inner text rather than collapsing to the placeholder. Pinned by TEST-27's `\( x \)` case.

Net improvement: the conversation card's accessible name is the display label, so a screen reader previously read the backslash-delimiter noise aloud. The header's edit INPUT is still seeded from the raw stored title (form.setValue at :63), so the strip affects only how the title READS, never what a user edits or saves — checked explicitly because a display transform leaking into an editable field would silently rewrite stored data on save.

No new sink: the output is rendered as React text content (escaped) and used in aria-label/tooltip/search, never via dangerouslySetInnerHTML. The transform only deletes delimiters and unescapes punctuation — it cannot introduce markup that was not already inert text.

A LaTeX command inside a pair stays literal (`\( \lambda \)` reads `\lambda`, not λ). Recorded in the module header as a deliberate limit rather than left as a surprise: mapping commands needs a real TeX table, and a partial map that silently drops unmappable symbols reads worse than showing them. The cases models actually emit inline — a bare symbol, a short relation — read correctly.
